critic_raw:
1. WEARABLE DATA AVAILABILITY AFTER DISCHARGE IS NOT VALIDATED AS A REALISTIC OPERATIONAL ASSUMPTION. The hypothesis requires that wearable device data is available for prediction of 30-day readmission risk. The design does not address whether patients discharged from hospital will continue wearing the device at home, whether compliance varies by demographic or diagnosis, or at what point during the post-discharge period the prediction is made. If wearable data coverage is sparse or biased (sicker patients less likely to wear devices consistently), the model is trained on a non-representative sample and cannot be deployed to the full patient population. The fundamental precondition of the hypothesis — that wearable time-series data is available at prediction time — is assumed but not validated. Evidence: report wearable device compliance rates by demographic group and diagnosis, and characterize what fraction of patients have sufficient post-discharge wearable coverage to make a prediction.

2. THE NEURAL NETWORK ARCHITECTURE FOR WEARABLE DATA IS NOT SPECIFIED. The design refers to 'a neural network model' that 'manages time-series intricacies' without specifying the architecture. For wearable data (heart rate, activity level at potentially minute-level resolution), the choice of architecture — CNN with sliding windows, LSTM, transformer, or attention-based model — fundamentally affects what temporal patterns are captured and at what time scale. The receptive field, window size, and downsampling strategy are all unspecified. A fully connected network that aggregates wearable statistics is very different from an LSTM that processes raw sequences. The claim that the model 'extracts nuanced insights from dynamic patterns' cannot be evaluated without knowing the architecture. Evidence: specify the model architecture and justify the choice for the wearable data characteristics.

3. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON ONE YEAR OF PATIENT DATA WITH 30-DAY LABELS CREATES LABEL OVERLAP BETWEEN PARTITIONS. The 30-day readmission label means that a patient discharged on day 330 of the year has a readmission window that extends to day 360. If the dataset is split randomly, patients discharged in overlapping 30-day windows at the partition boundary will have labels that reference the same temporal period as patients in the other partition. This is not classical leakage but it produces correlation between train and test label windows. More importantly, with only 10,000 patients over one year, the 70/15/15 split produces a test set of approximately 1,500 patients — a sample too small for reliable subgroup analysis. Evidence: report confidence intervals on AUC for the test set; with 1,500 patients and low readmission prevalence, the confidence intervals may be wide enough to make the result inconclusive.

4. THE LOGISTIC REGRESSION BASELINE USING ONLY 'CLINICAL DEMOGRAPHICS' DOES NOT REPRESENT THE PRACTITIONER INCUMBENT. Standard 30-day readmission prediction tools (LACE index, HOSPITAL score) combine clinical features, length of stay, acuity, and comorbidity indices. A logistic regression on demographics alone is a weak baseline that does not represent what clinicians already have. If the neural network beats demographics-only logistic regression but not LACE, the hypothesis is not supported at the level needed for clinical adoption. Evidence: include a validated clinical readmission risk score (LACE or HOSPITAL) as an additional baseline.

5. THE ASSUMPTION THAT 'PRIMARY SIGNAL IS CROSS-SECTIONAL' FOR WEARABLE READMISSION DATA IS NOT PHYSICALLY JUSTIFIED. The design justifies the random split by claiming 'the primary signal is cross-sectional.' For wearable data measuring heart rate and activity after hospital discharge, the temporal structure is the primary signal — the trajectory of heart rate recovery over days post-discharge is clinically meaningful, while the cross-sectional reading at any single moment is not. A cross-sectional model trained on wearable readings is missing the longitudinal recovery dynamics that constitute the hypothesis's mechanism. If the primary signal is truly cross-sectional, there is no advantage to using time-series data at all, which contradicts the hypothesis. Evidence: compare a model trained on the time-series trajectory to a model trained on a single snapshot reading; if performance is equivalent, the temporal signal is not providing the claimed advantage.
all_issues_raised: Wearable device compliance and data availability after discharge are not validated as operational prerequisites
Neural network architecture for wearable time-series is entirely unspecified
One-year dataset with 10,000 patients produces a test set too small for reliable subgroup analysis
Logistic regression on demographics-only is weaker than validated clinical readmission scores (LACE, HOSPITAL)
Cross-sectional signal assumption contradicts the temporal recovery mechanism the hypothesis depends on
Temporal alignment of wearable data relative to discharge and readmission events is unspecified — risk of label leakage if data spans the readmission event
Neural network receives wearable + demographic inputs while logistic regression receives only demographics — comparison tests information access, not architecture
Ten thousand records over one year may be insufficient for neural network training on irregular time-series, creating asymmetric sample size advantage for logistic regression
Random split across one year ignores seasonal readmission rate variation that affects evaluation stability
Pre-aggregation of wearable time-series into summary statistics negates the temporal modeling motivation for neural network — comparison reduces to tabular classification
One year of data is insufficient to separate seasonal readmission patterns from model signal under random split
Wearable compliance post-discharge is correlated with patient health status, creating a biased data availability mechanism
Aggregation window for wearable features is an unvalidated hyperparameter that inflates apparent model selection integrity
Modality (wearable vs. demographic) and architecture (neural network vs. logistic regression) are confounded in a 2x1 comparison
AUROC as primary metric does not align with the stated operational goal of intervention targeting at a specific risk threshold
